speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five. This is an interview with Dr. Eric Nash as done by Scott Fried on May 26th, 1995 in Forest Hills, New York, United States of America, conducted in English. OK, Dr. Nash--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thinking back of the miseries of Kaufering and slave labor camps of Dachau, what made us hold on to this miserable, miserable life? As far as I'm concerned, I was determined from the moment I left Auschwitz that I want to stay alive. I did everything possible because I wanted, more than anything else, to be reunited with my family. And this was the only thing-- this futile thing-- that kept me keeping this up. It was so much easier to give up. You could just sit down and become a muselmann and you just die. It was so much easier. It was much harder to stay alive. But when we finally, on the 20th of April, were-- came the appel, which was our last appel in a slave labor camp. We were put in formation, and we were marched out of the camp and onto a road which led through Landsberg itself. And lo and behold, we already had forgotten that there is a world around us with streets, with houses, with people living in there, living a fairly normal life. And the people who were lining the streets saw us, and we must have been a sight to see because they cried like little children. We were a bunch of walking skeletons in these filthy uniforms, infested with lice, covered with sores, barely on our feet. We must have been quite a sight to see and they, for the first time, saw what was going on in Hitler Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you feel that they didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You feel that these German people didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: As Thomas Mann said, they held their noses when they smelled the stench of the chimneys. I think they knew, but when they saw it-- when they were facing it face to face-- it was quite a shock for them. Maybe some of them really didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were chased for seven long, endless, days on the march down to Austria, which was called the death march. And without going into details, a lot of killing was going on on the march and barely a third of us who started out survived when we finally, on May 2nd, were liberated by the American army. We were completely exhausted when we saw the first Jeep, which we never saw in our lives before. We didn't know that such vehicles existed. And the strangest part of all was that we were liberated by Japanese soldiers, and we were always under the impression the Japanese were in a war against the Allies. It happened to be a Nisei regiment, and they turned out to be the loveliest, most wonderful people, who really helped us a great deal over these difficult days after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a feeling of ecstasy or relief?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no room for ecstasy. We were completely exhausted. We were deadly worried what happened to the rest of our family. And we were sick, all of us, physically and mentally. Of course, the first thing was that we ate ourselves sick, and most of us came down with dysentery from this food that we were not used to have. I myself came into a hospital, and I had a heart muscle damage called myocarditis, and I was in the hospital for a few weeks. And then the three of us organized a car, and we went back to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: To search for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- to search for my family. And only then we found out the bitter and horrible truth because one of my colleagues, Dr. [? Popper, ?] was a scribe in Auschwitz and he gave us all the gruesome details about the gas chambers and about the modalities, and gave us the certainty that none of my family was still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even at liberation, you, you didn't know what had really happened on the whole to European Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were always holding on to this futile hope. The hope became dimmer and dimmer, but you know, human nature is very strange as far as optimism is concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the, I guess, the feeling amongst the people in Czechoslovakia in your home town when you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then, the new republic has been established. It was, of course, nothing compared with the republic we had left and we were used to. Nevertheless, we were treated very decently, and we were given certain privileges as being freed prisoners. There was not much to be had at that time. There was not enough food, not enough clothing, but so you had to use some ingenuity to survive even at that time. But it was-- for us, it was nothing compared. We were trained in survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Czechoslovakia and tried to rebuild your life there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Czechoslovakia, and I recovered some of my documents and papers. And I was invited by the government to settle in a high medical position in the Sudetenland. The Germans were chased out of the Sudetenland, and it was completely empty. There were just a few Czechs there, and we were-- I was given a position with a practice in a large industrial town called Rumburk. And I looked at it, and I was willing to go there and to live there when my cousin, who lived in America, discovered my name on a survivor list and she immediately sent a telegram and arranged for all of the papers I should come to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I could take you back for a second, did you-- when you were liberated there were, I assume, SS men there and Germans there. Did you seek revenge or have that feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hate to talk about it, but yes we did. When we liberated we were barely able to stand up, so we were in no physical condition to do anything. But our hate and our lust for revenge was just there. After having gone through this almost three years of indescribable torture, you develop a state of hate which just knows no boundary. And when we got a little more strength, we went out and got a hold of two kapos, and they were actually hacked to death. We could not get a hold of any SS people because they were either by now in hiding, or they were caught by the Americans and put into camps. So we had to get a hold of whoever we could get a hold of. And even here, disappointment struck because one guy we had in our site and were really looking for all of a sudden turned out to be an adjutant of an American officer. He was armed, like we all were, with a gun, and he was in a position where he was untouchable to us. We would have loved to get a hold of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so now you were saying-- was it your cousin in America brought you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin in America, who lived in Buffalo, New York, arranged for my papers and for my visa. And on July 10th, 1946, I came to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wanted to come to America? Was that your first choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time I came for a visit?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't intend to necessarily emigrate.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What make you choose to emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I liked what I saw, and particularly what's-- how the medical field was organized. And I went for my tests to have my medical degree validated here, and I took a job. When I had one of my tests I met a comrade in arms who was with me in the army, in the Czech army. And he only heard that I was shot in the camp, and he became pale like anything when he saw me alive. And he had a job in Flushing Hospital and he said, you come with me and I'll get you a job there, and I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of other places, like the new Israel or Palestine at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I found some relative of my late wife here, and I felt pretty much at home here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked at Flushing Hospital, and then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was first an intern, later a resident in Flushing Hospital, and afterwards I offered-- opened my office in Forest Hills, and I still have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your present wife here, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my present wife here, who was also a refugee from Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: She too was a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in Paris, France, when the Germans invaded France, and her stepfather was in the Czech army, which was located in London at that time. And because he was in the army, they boarded a ship in Bordeaux, France and were shipped to England, and survived the war there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've had several-- a few children too, here, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1951, and I have two children, and I have four grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you ever returned to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact, I went to Auschwitz last year. I went to Auschwitz like people would go to a cemetery to visit their loved ones' graves. My loved ones have no graves, but I went to Auschwitz. I found the barracks I was in, and I took a piece of the ramp home, a stone from the ramp because the ramp was the last place I saw my wife and my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I-- if I might ask you, what is your opinion on, basically, the situation now in Europe? With the, I guess, fall of the Eastern Bloc nations, there has been a rise of anti-Semitism again. Do you-- do you fear that this tragedy could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is very hard to answer. I personally don't think such a thing can happen again in Europe. It's hard to claim all this when you hear what was going on in the Argentines, what was going on in Bosnia, but there is a big, basic difference. We were never in open rebellion. We were not an armed revolution. We were not threatening an overthrow of the government. We were singled out to be killed because we were Jews. And we only wanted to live and to breathe, and we were not allowed to do that. In Bosnia or in Argentina there was a kind of a civil war, so there was a war going on with all the miserable atrocities a war brings with it. As far as the neo-Nazi movement is concerned, you can only be a Nazi if you are either mentally deranged or if you're ignorant. The ignorance can be because you just don't know anything, or the ignorance can be on purpose-- that you don't want to know anything. I think the world has learned a lesson from the Holocaust, and I think it is our duty to spread and to report the news of the Holocaust against these ridiculous revisionists who will claim that it never existed. This is a picture of my first wife taken in 1938, before the war and around the time when we got married. And of course, there is no picture of our son. This is my sister, Trude, with her husband, Henry. They came with me to Theresienstadt, and they were shipped out to Ibiza where they perished. This is my brother, my older brother, a lawyer who went long before us on the transport and was executed in Minsk, Russia. This is my brother-- well this happens to be his passport from Czechoslovakia. That's the only picture that I have of my brothers. This is a picture of myself when I was young, and after liberation. This is my wife. We got married in 1951. This picture was taken in about 1965. We have two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Liliane Nash. This is my son, Thomas William Nash. The name Thomas is after our first Czech President, Thomas G. Masaryk. His middle initial is William, after my brother, killed in Minsk. He's a doctor, and this picture was taken about two years ago, in 1992. This is my daughter, Margaret Irma Nash. We call her Peggy Nash, and she doesn't like her middle initial very much, but she is named after my mother who perished in Auschwitz. She is married now to Mr. Edward Marks and his three lovely children.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my wife, Liliane with our daughter Peggy, taken on one of our vacations in Europe, about 1960. This is my daughter, Peggy, with her first child. This was taken about two years ago. Her name-- what? His name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Gregory, Gregory Marks. This is Katie Nash. Katie is the daughter of my son, Thomas Nash. She is now seven years old, and this was taken about a year ago. These are the three children of my daughter, Peggy. Their name is Gregory, the oldest, Stephanie, the princess in the middle, and last but not least, Michael. Michael is named after my murdered son, Misha. They all carry a middle initial N, which stands for Nash.
